data.title: ASML: Shares Could Get Way Cheaper
data.description:
ASML Holding N.V. (ASML) is down by over 6% since reporting Q1 2025 earnings. Even though the company guided $4.3 billion below the analyst consensus for FY 2025, margins for the full year were reaffirmed at 51% - 53%.

I believe the market's overreaction was mainly due to the uncertainty surrounding tariffs (both reciprocal and sectoral). CFO Roger Dassen said in the earnings call that they "are currently facing an elevated level of uncertainty surrounding tariffs, which may have both direct and indirect implications for our business".

Will tariffs on semiconductors affect EUV machines? Will these secotral tariffs add on top of the reciprocal tariffs revealed on April 2? Will the US and the EU reach an agreement before the deadline? Will the EU retailiate if no agreement is reached?

All these unanswered questions are putting pressure on the stock.

At the midpoint, the guided gross margin for Q2 is 250 bps below Q1's 54%. The Q2 revenue guidance also fell short of the Street's expectations by $1.35 billion.

Sales from China, tariffs, and the demand for EUV and DUV tools were some of the hot topics in the earnings call. Net system sales in China for FY 2025 have now been revised upwards to ~25% from the initial expectation of 20s. From a demand perspective, the company still sees this year and next year as growth periods, mainly due to rising AI demand in both logic and memory, which hasn't changed (according to the CFO) due to the tariff drama in the US.

Considering that the new administration in the US is about to clarify sectoral tariffs on semiconductors, I wouldn't jump to buy this stock yet. I believe the announcement will tank semiconductor stocks around the world, unless the tariff comes in below 25%.

I will be direct. I believe this tariff (on semis) will be short lived, similarly to the tariff on autos. I expect to see a global 10% tariff on all products, except those from China, which may face even higher tariff rates. This 10% cost will likely be passed on to US customers, including Intel, without much trouble.

That said, my Hold rating hasn't improved yet.

Strong Sales In China

Given my early concerns with the revenue in China, I was highly anticipating the earnings season to see any followups on this topic.

I have to say that I was pleasantly surprised by the management's outlook for China sales this year. The initial estimate was for low 20s of net system sales. This value was now increased to >25%. So far, it looks like export control restrictions in the region haven't affected DUV system (beyond the NXT:1950 models). So far, the company is still able to sell the 1950 and 1940 models in China, which are capable 28 nm production. As a side note, the revenue mix from China was similar to Q4 2024, representing 27% of system sales coming from this region. That's above the US, which came in at 16% in the past quarter, down from 28% in Q4 2024.

Despite the ongoing tariff drama and export control rules limiting sales in China, the company has still confirmed its long term revenue goal of EUR 44 billion to 60 billion by 2030, with a gross margin between 56 % - 60%. In my view, their 2030 target isn’t just part of a typical five year corporate plan. It seems tied to their expectations for strong results in both sales and margins from the NXE:5200 high NA EUV tools. More on this topic in the next sections.

The Impact Of Tairffs

Here’s how CFO Roger Dassen explained the drop in margins for Q2 2025:

The gross margin in the second half of the year is expected to be lower than the first half, primarily due to the expected margin dilutive effect of the revenue recognition of High-NA systems in the second half of the year, lower upgrade revenue, as well as any potential impact of tariffs.

As always, I tend to read these justifications backwards by order of importance.

First, let's put things into perspective. Q1 2025 gross margin came in at 54%. The guidance for Q2 is between 50% and 53%. At the midpoint, this is a decrease of 250 bps.

Now, let's try to gauge the impact that tariffs had on the company. As you may know, there is a tariff pause for the next ~80 days. Tariff pause doesn't mean no tariffs. The base rate is 10% for every country in the infamous list presented on Liberation day. As for Europe, Von der Leyen pointed to retaliatory tariffs, although after the Italian prime minister visit to the White House, it all points out that a deal between the US and EU will be reached.

Let's assume we have no deal, and both the US and EU set a 20% tariff on imports. Also, let's assume that the upcoming sectoral tariffs on semiconductors will disappear (similarly to what may happen to autos) before the 90 day pause (I have a high conviction of this scenario). What would be ASML's impact?

A quick look at the latest 20-F reveals an interesting slide where one can see ASML's supply chain distribution.

As seen above, the US accounts for 27% of all suppliers. Let's assume that half of COGS last year (cost of goods sold) accounted for materials ($14.27 billion / 2 = $7.14 billion). This leads to $1.43 billion of additional expenses due a hypothetical 20% retaliatory EU tariff.

From a sales perspective, US revenue accounted for 16% of total net sales last year.

Assuming the company is able to pass 50% of the tariff to customers, the expected impact would be $452 million. That's a third compared to the tariff impact on importing US products into the EU.

Overall, the impact of 20% bilateral tariffs between the US and the EU would be ~$2 billion, or 21% of last year's net earnings of $9.42 billion.

This is the worst case scenario, by the way.

In a realistic scenario, we’d likely see a 10 percent tariff on US imports, and I believe that cost would be fully passed on to customers. In fact, for FY 2025, the company has reiterated their margin guidance of 51% - 53%, with "the caveat of the certainties around tariffs", according to the CFO in his prepared remarks. Furthermore, in the Q&A, the CFO said:

the burden of that [the tariff on US imports] should not be with ASML, that the burden of the tariff, that the lion's share of the tariff burden should be borne by the next element in the value chain.

Seeing this guidance for the full year, I certainly belive that the company expects the scenario mentioned above: a universal 10% tariff, which will be fully passed on to US customers (Intel).

That said, the upcoming announcement of sector specific tariffs on semiconductors will likely hurt ASML's stock. This is the main reason I don’t think it’s a good time to buy.

High-NA EUV Adoption

Let's clarify the jargon first.

High-NA stands for high numerical aperture. The higher the NA, the smaller the features you can print on a silicon wafer, which is great news if you are planning build 2nm and beyond chips. The current generation of EUV tools has a NA of ~0.33, whereas the next generation (NXE:5000/NXE5200) has ~0.55.

In terms of average selling prices per machine, the company disclosed an ASP for the high NA tools of $380 million. In the latest Q&A, management mentioned that low NA EUV ASPs (NXE:3800E/3600) are currently around €227M, with a gross margin above the the corporate gross margin (54% in Q1 2025).

That said, we won't see mass production of these machines in the near term. The CEO noted three phases for the commercialization of high-NA EUV tools. The first one, is R&D. This is where the company and its customers is today, after shipping its 5th and last NXE:5000 machine in Q1. Last year, the company was able to recognize revenue for two of these machines and for 2025, the expectation is for five more. Interestingly enough, not all 5 will be for the R&D model (i.e., the NXE:5000). Some (no specific number given) will include the NXE:5200.

The next phase includes volume production testing with the NXE:5200 machine. This could last anywhere between 2-3 years before we see the final phase: high volume production.

And once this is done, typically, a customer will be convinced that the tool can do the job, the tool is mature. And then they will use it fully for any future node in high volume manufacturing. And that, I think, we have said will come mostly '27, '28.

So far, there are three customers that have adopted these tools: Samsung, Intel, and an unnamed third customer, that I suspect may be TSMC.

Even though I see positive progress, with improvements noted by both Intel and Samsung, the company is still several years away from seeing significant results from the sale of high NA EUV tools.

Conclusion

To wrap up, I see the current uncertainty around sectoral and reciprocal tariffs weighing on the stock. On the positive side, strong China sales despite export control restrictions and solid margin guidance for FY 2025 are encouraging. On the negative side, the upcoming tariff announcement on semis could trigger another selloff next week.

The company’s long term outlook is intact, but in the near term, there is too much policy risk to justify a buy rating. I reiterate my hold rating for now until the tariff situation becomes clearer.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Don't Be Bamboozled By The Bears Again
data.description:
We ended another tumultuous week with stocks meandering for most of Thursday. The one exception was UnitedHealth (UNH), which suffered a 22% decline from an earnings shortfall, stinging the Dow Jones Industrial Average for a 500-plus point loss. Otherwise, it was a day of relative calm compared to the past two weeks, which are breathing new life into a bearish narrative that was unsuccessful in 2023, as well as 2024. The bears are at it again, but I advise not being bamboozled by their rhetoric. Despite the debacle caused by the rollout of the Trump administration's trade policies, a recession and bear market are not foregone conclusions.

The correction that most of all market strategists at the beginning of this year expected, including myself, came fast and furious after the S&P 500 clinched its last all-time high on February 19 at 6,144. It was led by an overvalued technology sector with its largest and most dominant companies, otherwise known as the Magnificent 7, experiencing several quarters of decelerating earnings growth rates. The writing was on the wall. Yet at the same time, the remaining 493 names were just starting to see earnings growth increase. This occurred with the economic backdrop of a mid-cycle slowdown on tap and orchestrated by the Federal Reserve through its increase in short-term interest rates from near zero to a peak of 5.25% over the past two years.

A soft landing was and still is in sight, as both headline and core rates for the Consumer Price Index [CPI] and personal consumption expenditures [PCE] price index have 2-handles through March of this year. While Chairman Powell was admittedly late responding to the inflationary spiral that started at the beginning of 2021, his effort to lower the inflation rate from its peak in 2022 without derailing the economic expansion and driving unemployment higher has been very successful. If it were not for the lagging decline in shelter costs, due to the way it is calculated, we would already be at 2% inflation rate.

Additionally, the rate of economic growth held above trend for the past two years, expanding 2.8% in 2024, and the economy was on a strong footing to begin 2025 with low unemployment and steady real wage growth. That said, we were expecting the rate to slow this year, due largely to the lagged impact of tighter monetary policy in 2024. Still, corporate earnings growth was expected to broaden and strengthen to double-digits in 2025.

The correction that took place late February and early March was a normal and healthy one to rid the markets of the speculative excesses in AI-related technology names and other corners of the market that were clearly getting ahead of themselves, but the average stock was not excessively valued. The huge weighting that a handful of very expensive names had on market-cap weighted indexes like the S&P 500 (SPY) and Nasdaq 100 (QQQ) made the overall market look a lot more expensive than it was on average.

The initial correction morphed into a much deeper drawdown on "Liberation Day" with the announcement of the president's reciprocal tariff program, because the tariffs were clearly not reciprocal, as advertised. A 10% correction turned into a narrow miss of a bear market with an 18.9% decline from the all-time high. Despite recovering most of the losses that occurred in the three days that followed Liberation Day, the bears would have us think that this peak to trough decline validates the same bearish narrative that has been incorrectly forecasting a recession and bear market ever since the bull was born in October 2022.

Instead of claiming that the disinflationary trend would end, or that valuations were too high, or that the economy was on the cusp of a recession, the new-found pessimism is grounded in an outlook for stagflation. Granted, the rate of economic growth will slow, as it was scheduled to do before trade policy was introduced. I will also concede that tariffs are inflationary because they increase prices for producers and consumers. Lastly, if the tariffs are implemented as proposed, they will not only increase prices and slow growth but likely lead to higher unemployment. That is the definition of stagflation, but it assumes that the most recently proposed effective tariff rate is enacted. I think most definitely it will not.

The reason is that it would be political suicide for the president, and I think he now understands this. The reports on how tariffs will burden US businesses and consumers have been relentless the past two weeks. Consumer, business, and investor sentiment has plummeted. Members of Congress, who support the president's agenda, are looking for cover. Chairman Powell has indicated he has no intentions of lowering short-term rates, as the president is demanding, to offset the higher inflation and headwinds to growth that tariffs present. Even if Powell adhered to the president's demands, lower short-term rates would do little to offset these headwinds, as it is long-term rates that dictate borrowing costs for consumers. In fact, I have never seen the adverse consequences of a policy so widely advertised and broadly feared by the consensus as this one is today.

For this reason, I am extremely confident that we will see a significantly scaled down version of the reciprocal tariff program, which in some cases leads to no tariffs at all. Of course, this entire process will cause some long-term damage that is irreversible, but if I am correct in that tariffs are replaced with trade deals in the coming weeks, then this expansion can continue, we can avoid stagflation, as well as a deeper drawdown in the S&P 500 that results in a bear market.

data.title: Archer Aviation: I Remain Bullish
data.description:
In February, I covered Archer Aviation (ACHR) with a buy rating after the company raised additional funds. In my view, capital raises will recur until Archer Aviation has certified its Midnight eVTOL and has stabilized production. So, this is a name that is a speculative buy. The stock price has lost nearly 23% of its value since then compared to a 12.3% decline for the S&P 500. In this report, I will discuss the stock price decline, the 2024 cash burn, and update my price target.

Why Did Archer Aviation Stock Decline?

Archer Aviation did not completely follow the movements of the S&P 500. However, I have not observed any items specific to Archer Aviation that explain the sell-off. Instead, I believe that the market has positioned itself somewhat differently, and that doesn't benefit Archer Aviation.

Recession fears have risen and that could ultimately impact Archer Aviation. Currently, there is no indication that customers are rethinking their eVTOL plans, but it is likely that in case a recession does occur that some customers will. At the same time, there have been very few customers or potential customers that have outlined clear plans. So, the fear is that some of the potential sales in the pipeline may never materialize.

The other explanation is that due to the uncertainty, investors have grown somewhat more risk-adverse and that does not favor names such as Archer Aviation. We should not forget that Archer Aviation is developing an innovative product in a new transportation mode and for the development, certification and initial production, significant amounts of cash are required. These amounts are currently collected through capital raises. So, that is simply not the combination that investors like in an uncertain environment. The stock price decline, in my view, has not been driven by any change in the planning.

Furthermore, there could be an impact on the cost of goods due to the implementation of tariffs. So, the risk has risen, and the market has become more risk-averse. I believe that sums up why the stock price has declined.

Archer Aviation Bolsters Liquidity And Prepares For Service Entry

Archer Aviation ended the year with $841.3 million in cash and cash equivalents. The company burned $368.6 million in operating activities and had $82 million in investing cash burn bringing its free cash flow burn to roughly $450 million, which was fully covered through the issuance of $758.8 million of common stock. So, the company covered its cash burn and raised a substantial amount of cash to cover cash burns in 2025.

The capital raise in February would raise the pro forma cash position to more than $1 billion, which is extremely strong.

While the stock has fallen, there are positives for Archer Aviation:

  • Archer Aviation has commenced production of its first Midnight aircraft with plans to produce a total of 10 units this year in Covington.
  • The company is positioned well to capitalize on opportunities for eVTOLs in defense.
  • Archer Aviation has signed its first two customers for the Launch Edition program, which aims to support customers in bringing the Midnight to commercial service.
  • The company is still aiming to bring a taxi service to New York with United Airlines (UAL) this year.

So, there is a lot to be positive about. I especially like the Launch Edition signing with Ethiopian Airlines. The program is valued at $30 million and shows there are possibilities to capitalize beyond aircraft production and spare parts. Furthermore, it does show that eVTOLs can be deployed in regions with lower GDP per capita and perhaps also signals that urban air mobility can be deployed in areas where infrastructure is less advanced.

What Is Archer Aviation Stock Worth?

To calculate the stock price target for Archer Aviation, I am using a method that is based on the book value. The major moving item for the book value per share is the cash burn and the capital raises, which increase the number of shares outstanding.

To calculate the cash flows, I use a combination of the historical trends and comments from Archer Aviation, we’re not just projecting out based on current trends as shown in the trendlines. Instead, we use the dashed assumptions in the graph. For the first quarter of 2025, the company is expecting adjusted EBITDA between $95 million and $110 million.

For CapEx, the company provided the following comment during a recent earnings call:

We expect the spend on supplier non-recurring engineering costs and Archer CapEx to be down in 2025, since in 2024 we have invested heavily in this area.

This leads to a cash burn estimate of $430 million to $550 million, which is an $80 million increase at the low-end compared to my prior estimate. I am not trying to provide the rosiest picture, but implement the more demanding estimates to assess whether the stock remains attractive.

Based on these inputs, I expect that the cash position by the end of this year will be $976.3 million on the assumption that a $390 million raise from Stellantis will be finalized.

I believe that the cash per share will decline from $1.66 to $1.63, but the book value per share would remain more or less table at $1.49. The median forward P/B for industrials is 2.7x, which would bring the price target to $3.98 per share and by using the forward multiple of 5.3x for Archer Aviation, we would get to a price target of $7.95 providing 10% upside. A peer like Joby (JOBY) has a multiple of 5.1x, while Eve (EVEX) even trades at 8.7x forward P/B. So, 5.3x is high compared to other industrials, but looking at eVTOL peers it is not extremely high.

Conclusion: Archer Aviation Remains An Interesting Bet

I believe that Archer Aviation remains a fascinating stock. It is definitely not a stock that is cheap or risk-free. If you buy, you have to accept the risk that comes with it. However, while the stock price has declined, we are seeing some derisking elements as the company is getting closer to securing additional funding, and it is closer to service entry of the Midnight. That should start bringing in more and more down payments and reduce the need for capital raises.

I believe the stock is not cheap to buy now, but it might never be. So, this is a speculative buy with a $7.95 price target based on the projections made for 2025.

data.title: The Metals Company: These Are Not The Metals Trump Is Looking For
data.description:
The Metals Company (NASDAQ:TMC) is pursuing a novel approach to mining critical mineral resources through deep-sea nodule mining. The company launched as an SPAC with limited initial capital. The technology is not yet proven at a large commercial scale, but it has the potential to enable the US access to a massive mineral deposit in the Clarion-Clipperton zone, located around Hawaii to Mexico.

Following its SPAC launch results and the historical failure of its predecessor, Nautilus Minerals gave TMC a poor outlook, causing TMC to lose around half of its value until the end of last year. However, since December, TMC has been on a streak, rising over 200% on speculation that the Trump administration will benefit the company as it seeks to secure rare earth minerals. This is of increased importance this week, following China's ban on all rare earth mineral exports to the US, which has a near-monopoly on the market, and threatens the numerous industries that depend on it.

In January, I published a neutral view on the stock in "TMC the metals company: Facing Liquidity Crisis As It Bleeds Into Unproven Technologies." My view was that the company faced significant challenges in securing capital for investment and addressing environmental controversies surrounding its novel mining process. I also pointed out that most rare earth minerals were, in fact, very cheap at that time compared to their ten-year trading history.

If not for Trump's efforts, I would be bearish on the stock. However, the company was starting to receive increased speculation around more direct support from the Trump administration, giving me a neutral outlook. Indeed, that view is proving correct, as TMC has surged by a staggering 82% since I last covered it.

The fact is that the world today is very different from what it was in mid-January. There is far less concern that US policy will create environmental regulatory challenges for TMC. There is also a far greater need to secure rare earth minerals now that US-China trade has been effectively halted. In light of this, I expect TMC will have far lower barriers to accessing capital. That said, in the long run, its approach must still prove profitable and scalable.

Deep-Sea Mining's Fast Track to Growth

Approximately 70% of the US's rare earth imports originate from China. Certain minerals, such as yttrium and dysprosium, come almost exclusively from China. In general, China controls most of the global supply chain, particularly the processing of heavy rare earth elements, including the two above, as well as terbium, gadolinium, and other elements. The medium and light rare earth metals, such as neodymium, are more often mined outside of China (e.g., Australia), but are still generally processed in China.

The US has vast quantities of these minerals in the ground, but has only recently made an effort to create mines, and they cannot expand quickly enough to meet demand. China has a dominant presence in mining, but is even more dominant in heavy rare earth processing, which gives it control over the export of minerals mined in neighboring countries, such as Australia. China has also banned all exports of rare earth extraction and separation technologies, limiting the US's ability to build its supplies quickly.

This has created a race for investment, with a particular effort from the Trump administration. TMC surged this week after the President drafted an executive order enabling the stockpile of critical metals from the Pacific Ocean. Last month, Trump fast-tracked permitting for deep-sea mining in international waters, bypassing the United Nations' review process, although the US has never ratified the International Seabed Authority. In March, TMC CEO Gerared Barron announced a nearly $20M direct investment offering, as well as an effort to utilize the Deep Seabed Hard Mineral Resources Act, which is expected to grant permits much faster than the International Seabed Authority. Barron also noted milestones, including the processing of nodules containing copper, nickel, and cobalt alloys.

When I last covered TMC, I noted concerns regarding environmental regulations and funding. TMC only had around $3M in cash on hand at the end of last quarter, and the $20M capital raise likely only keeps it afloat for some time. However, its market capitalization is now over $1B, meaning it can relatively easily raise capital on the public market, given voracious investment demand for the stock. Furthermore, with Trump looking to stockpile minerals mined in the Pacific Ocean, and given that TMC is the largest company pursuing this (even if still at a small scale), it has a likely profitable market for its operations.

The US executive branch does not share the concerns of the previous administration regarding environmental risk. That debate centers on the lack of a public oversight system and the unclear ecological risks associated with potentially introducing heavy metals into the ocean, which could harm marine life and food supplies. Other analyses suggest that risk is primarily limited to the deep sea, and the known environmental risks associated with land-based rare earth mining are likely to be more severe. It is worth noting that TMC's technology maintains seafloor plumes at 5-6 meters, rather than many kilometers, as with previous technologies, which likely limits environmental risk. Regardless, at least in the short term, with Trump's acceleration efforts, I do not expect regulations to create barriers for the firm.

What is TMC's Long-Term Value Proposition?

The Metals Company is focused on high grades of four critical metals: nickel, copper, cobalt, and manganese. None of these are rare earth metals subject to China's export ban. However, China is stockpiling these metals, particularly due to issues shared with the US regarding export volatility from the Congo and other unstable countries in Central Africa. Still, although the cobalt price surged for that reason, it, like nickel, remains historically cheap:

As I've discussed in many recent articles, global manufacturing demand is slower today than it was years ago. This issue is generally expected to accelerate with tariffs that will create bottlenecks in the US and Chinese manufacturing supply chains, while raising prices for customers and lowering demand. Based on their lower prices, there is no acute shortage of either mineral. Manganese is also historically cheap. Copper is elevated, but its decline is accelerating with the growing recession signals.

As with rare-earth elements, the US has large deposits of minerals that will be used to produce the resources TMC will utilize. The US recently built a cobalt mine, but it closed before operations because the cobalt price is too low. The US has nickel resources and one operating mine, but these mines also face profitability issues today. Copper is very abundant in Chile, which has been a source of stable exports to the US. Manganese is very inexpensive, costing only a few dollars per ton, so it is hardly mined in the US. However, there is a wide range of exporters in Africa, Australia, and South America.

Despite headlines, from what I've read, TMC is not looking to produce rare earth minerals; it is looking to mine the four critical metals used in batteries, none of which are subject to China's export ban. In my view, this begs the question: Is The Metals Company a solution (or non-solution) to a non-issue? There is considerable speculation surrounding it, as well as the need for heavy rare earth metals; however, it lacks access to or processing operations for rare earth metals, which China currently monopolizes.

Based on an assessment of its Nori-D findings, it appears that there may be minute amounts of rare earth metals in some deep-sea nodules, with infinitesimally small grade percentages (under 1% of 1%). As far as I've researched, the company has not disclosed any plans to profit from rare earth elements and is instead focused on critical metals, particularly nickel, cobalt, copper, and manganese, none of which are currently in an acute supply shortage or are extremely China-dependent.

These minerals are not in apparent shortage and can be mined on US land if prices rise enough to make it profitable. It is unclear what the specific benefit of deep-sea mining these metals is, besides potentially less regulatory oversight. The last investor call noted that these metals can also be mined in a single location, rather than in separate mines, which may be an economic benefit. These deep-sea rocks are the size of potatoes, and the metals they contain are mostly manganese, which is worth very little per ton, comparable to iron. The vast majority of TMC's potential revenues come from the 1-1.5% of the nodules made of Nickel and Cobalt, neither of which is in an acute shortage today.

The Bottom Line

At this point, I am willing to drop two of my previous concerns with the company. In light of Trump's efforts to accelerate deep-sea mining, I am not concerned about TMC's ability to obtain permits or raise initial development capital, despite its low cash on hand.

That said, from a long-term business perspective, I do not see a profit opportunity in TMC. For one, its primary revenue potential comes from minerals that can be mined on land using much more well-estbaliedh technologies that I expect are more profitable. Furthermore, although demand for these specific minerals is likely to rise with the growth of EV battery production, they're not currently in acute shortage, and prices are relatively low.

The Metals Company provides an in-depth analysis of its ability to mine and process these alloys on a small scale, highlighting the likely low environmental risk associated with this approach. The company also reveals that the Clarion-Clipperton Zone seafloor, which is remarkably vast, spanning the width of the US, has a substantial total implied supply of these minerals. The company is also working on its feasibility study, which will determine how to build mines and assess its ability to scale production.

Although one of its recent investor presentation notes an EBITDA margin projection of 60%, the document cited for that point dates back to 2021, before the significant inflationary increase in mining costs. That document also does not clearly explain how it made operating cost and capital investment assumptions. However, it did note a high capital cost estimate of $10.6B, far beyond TMC's current market capitalization. Detractors argue that deep-sea mining is unlikely to be competitively profitable. The fact is that TMC is nowhere near the scale at which its profitability potential can accurately be determined, and I speculate that it is not necessarily a cheaper mining resource than onshore. Further, it faces a vague timeline, capital investment cost, and engineering feasibility in launching its operations.

Trump can create a market for Pacific minerals through the stockpile effort. The administration can also accelerate TMC's growth by lowering regulatory burdens. This shift has significantly improved capital investment potential by substantially increasing TMC's value. In my view, Trump is willing to consider almost any measure to secure more access to critical and rare earth minerals, even efforts that may not be feasible at scale or within the timeline Trump aims for. In my understanding, the US primarily lacks rare earth metal processing, but it does have land deposits of these metals. TMC promises a supply of non-rare earth metals that are not in short supply, but may come at a high cost.

My outlook on TMC remains neutral, but I watch the stock with great interest. Although it is receiving much direct and indirect support, benefiting its stock price, I am not convinced that it can produce needed metals at the scale, time, and cost that Trump is ultimately looking for. To me, this can be done better by investing in domestic mines and processing facilities. However, there is no clear reason to be bearish on TMC as long as it continues to benefit from speculative hope in its success. Still, in the long run, I do not expect TMC will make a significant and sustained profit at an appropriate capital cost standpoint. Further, although Trump's policies may benefit it for the next ~4 years, its value may likely be compromised if the political pendulum swings again.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Abbott Labs' Rally Is Well Deserved - Fade The Rally For Now
data.description:
ABT Continues To Offer A Compelling High-Growth Investment Thesis Despite The Recent Rally – Patience Before Adding

We previously covered Abbott Laboratories (NYSE:ABT) in February 2024, discussing how it continued to ride the diabetes tailwinds, as the market turned increasingly optimistic about the synergistic use of Continuous Glucose Monitoring [CGM] and Ozempic/Mounjaro for overall patient care.

Combined with the management's promising FY2024 guidance, growing profit margins, and paying off R&D efforts, we reiterated our Buy rating then, thanks to the excellent dividend investment thesis and the prospective capital appreciation.

ABT 1Y Stock Price

Since then, ABT has had an excellent total return of +11.3%, well outperforming the wider market at +3.8% and its medical monitoring device peers in varying degrees, despite the market-wide selloff since mid-February 2025.

Much of the outperformance is naturally attributed to the growing medical devices and adult nutrition sales, as similarly observed in their robust organic sales increase by +13.4% YoY and +8.9% YoY in FY2024, along with +12.6% YoY and +8.7% YoY in FQ1 '25, respectively.

It is apparent that the growing adoption of GLP-1 therapies has directly contributed to the growing number of ABT's Libre users as "part of a companion therapy approach for managing their diabetes," with the side effect of rapid muscle mass loss also boosting the demand for its adult nutritional products.

With obesity drugs expected to reach over $130B in 2030 annual sales, expanding at a CAGR of +66.5%, we can understand why the market has gotten exuberant surrounding the company's bright long-term prospects, with the synergistic relationship between CGM products and weight loss similarly supported by scientific studies.

Its pediatric nutrition and established pharmaceuticals have outperformed as well, with organic sales growth by +3.7% YoY and +9.2% YoY in FY2024, along with +4.9% YoY and +7.8% YoY in FQ1'25, respectively, with these tailwinds well balancing the headwinds observed in the Diagnostic segment at -3.9% YoY and -4.9% YoY.

Therefore, despite the potential tariff impact of "a few $100 million" in FY2025, we can understand why ABT has felt confident to reiterate its FY2025 guidance in the FQ1'25 earnings call, against the original consideration of "raising our EPS guidance... prior to the whole tariffs"

This is with FY2025 organic sales growth guidance at +8% YoY at the midpoint (compared to +9.6% YoY in FY2024), adj operating margins of 23.75% (+1.5 points YoY), and adj EPS of $5.15 (+10.2% YoY).

The Consensus Forward Estimates

These reasons may also be why the consensus has further raised their forward estimates, with ABT expected to chart an accelerated top/bottom-line growth at a CAGR of +6.9%/+10.4% through FY2027, respectively.

This is compared to the original estimates of +6.5%/+9%, the 5Y historical growth at a CAGR of +5.6%/+7.6%, and 10Y historical growth at +7.6% +9%, respectively, with it underscoring its renewed growth opportunities thanks to the growing GLP-1 care.

If anything, readers must note that ABT's rich Free Cash Flow generation of $6.35B in FY2024 (+25.4% YoY) has directly triggered the drastically healthier balance sheet.

This is with a lower net debt of -$6.16B in FY2024 (-16.7% YoY/-55.3% from FY2019 levels of -$13.79B/-65.9% from peak FY2017 levels of -$18.11B), with the detailed FQ1 '25 financial reports yet available.

ABT Valuations

These reasons may also be why the market has awarded ABT with the premium FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 25.18x and FWD PEG non-GAAP ratio of 2.50x, compared to its 1Y mean of 23.15x/4.45x, 5Y mean of 25.16x/2.89x, and 10Y mean of 22.93x/2.54x, respectively.

On the other hand, it appears that ABT may be trading at a slight premium compared to the sector FWD PEG non-GAAP median ratio of 1.62x and its medical monitoring device peers, such as Medtronic plc (MDT) at FWD PEG non-GAAP ratio of 2.24x and DexCom, Inc. (DXCM) at 1.58x.

This development implies that a moderate pullback may be necessary for an improved return profile, one that we shall further discuss in the next segment.

So, Is ABT Stock A Buy, Sell, or Hold?

ABT 5Y Stock Price

For now, ABT has had a sustained uptrend rally by +38.5% since the October 2023 bottom, as the bulls defend the 100-day moving averages during the recent market-wide selloff.

Based on the reaffirmed FY2025 adj EPS of $5.15 at the midpoint (+10.2% YoY) and the 1Y P/E mean of 23.15x (nearer to its peer mean of 24x), it is apparent that the stock is trading at a slight premium to our fair value estimates of $119.20.

Based on the consensus FY2027 adj EPS estimates of $6.29, ABT has also pulled forward part of its upside potential to our long-term price target of $145.60, as the market grows exuberant surrounding its FQ1'25 outperformance and FY2025 prospects.

Thanks to growing profitability and the healthier balance sheet, the management has also been able to offer robust dividend per share growth by +7.2% during the last hike, while building upon the 10Y growth at a CAGR of +9.5%.

The richer forward dividend yields of 1.82%, compared to the 5Y average of 1.45% and the sector median of 1.69%, have allowed long-term shareholders to subscribe to a DRIP program while accumulating additional shares on a regular basis as well.

As a result of the promising growth prospects, we are cautiously reiterating our Buy rating for the ABT stock, albeit with the caveat that interested investors observe the stock price movement for a little longer and wait for a moderate retracement to between its 100-day moving averages of $123 and its 200-day moving averages of $117 for an improved margin of safety, with those levels also nearer to our fair value estimates.

Our caution is not overly pessimistic as well, since the new US administration's potential tariff on the pharmaceutical industry is likely to trigger moderate volatility.

This is on top of the 10% import tariff, as the International market comprises 61% of the company's FY2024 revenues (-0.4 points YoY) and China at 5% of the FY2024 revenues (-0.6 points YoY).

As a result of the potential stock price downside by -9% and the drastically higher short interest volumes by +50.4% on a YoY basis, we believe that patients may be prudent in the meantime.

data.title: Cavco Industries: Affordability Remains A Clear Competitive Edge
data.description:
Cavco Industries, Inc. (CVCO) has posted great earnings in recent quarters - despite lingering uncertainty in the housing market, the affordable factory-built home producer has even shown increasing financial momentum. With a strengthening demand outlook, Cavco has even been increasing capacity utilization. On the other hand, persisting high mortgage rates and tariffs may continue to pressure the industry’s prospects, now potentially carrying onto Cavco as well.

In my previous June 2024 article on the stock, “Cavco Industries: Fair Performance, Fair Valuation”, I downgraded my rating on Cavco to Hold based on a fair comparable valuation. Through a strong performance, the stock has since returned 40%, well above S&P 500’s decline of -3% since. Notably, Cavco’s stock has also performed much better than the industry’s SPDR Homebuilders ETF (XHB), which has depreciated by around -9% after the previous article.

Cavco’s Recent Financials Reflect Strong Affordable Housing Demand

Cavco’s financials have performed incredibly well in the latest two reported quarters. In the most recent 2025 fiscal Q3 period, Cavco managed to increase home sales units by 21.6% year-on-year to 5059 in spite of quite stagnant industry-wide new home sales growth for the quarter. The sales price per sold home did decline by -3.5% to $99.0 thousand, though, reflecting pressure in industry-wide average housing prices over the past couple of years.

The surging unit sales are very accretive to Cavco’s earnings – the company has been able to ramp up capacity utilization in production from 60% to 75% due to a healthy enough backlog and improving demand, raising homebuilding margins through operating leverage. The housing gross margin improved by 1.2 percentage points year-on-year to 23.6% in Q3 despite the noteworthy decline in the average sales price. With a great improvement in financial services earnings on top through underwriting changes and premium increases, Cavco’s total Q3 operating income ended up very strong at $64.0 million, up 61.0% year-on-year.

It seems that the previous decade’s surge in average housing prices is still contributing very well to demand for affordable housing. As Cavco’s average sales price of just $99.0 thousand for a factory-built home is just a fraction of the US median of $419.2 thousand, potential homebuyers’ financial strain may turn buyers increasingly into affordable housing. The housing affordability index has declined very notably in past years, and has stayed relatively subdued in recent months. For comparison, D.R. Horton (DHI) reported a -2% revenue decline in the late-2024 quarter and Lennar (LEN) reported a -9% homebuilding revenue decline, whereas PulteGroup (PHM) reported a 13% growth. All still performed worse than Cavco, still reflecting quite challenging industry conditions.

The Housing Market’s Outlook Remains Turbulent

The housing market’s outlook has lingering uncertainty. Critically, mortgage rates have remained high, and even surged in mid-April as bond yields have shot up. Cavco told in the Q3 earnings call that the company expects consumers to start to gradually adjust to the persisting higher interest rates, and Freddie Mac has noted a similar point. Still, Freddie Mac also points out higher mortgage rates may continue to impact prospective buyers, turning away prospective homebuyers.

Also, Trump’s tariffs may impact material costs throughout the industry, potentially also making Cavco’s affordable housing less affordable. For example, the levy on Canadian lumber is being increased to at least 34.5% from 14.5%. The increased levy could raise effective prices of wood for Cavco, which is a critical material in the company’s manufacturing. NAHB sees the levy as a way for the US to improve self-sufficiency, but improved domestic lumber production is likely to take months or even years – homebuilders could see much higher material costs for at least the short- to mid-term. NAHB has also noted the trade war’s potential negative impact on the economy despite the current lifting of baseline tariffs on Mexico and Canada.

Ultimately, I believe that Cavco should manage to navigate economic uncertainty well. The company’s LTM operating margin remains healthy at 9.8% and the company has nearly no interest-bearing debt. Further, the positioning in affordable housing should be relatively defensive compared to many other homebuilders – increased manufacturing costs from tariffs are likely to be passed to homebuyers at least partially, pressuring home affordability and in turn converting homebuyers into Cavco’s affordable factory-built options. A similar advantage from affordability should be seen regarding higher mortgage rates. The uncertainty could still very well cause notable earnings pressure on Cavco as well.

Cavco’s Valuation Is Starting to Get Unattractive

I updated my discounted cash flow [DCF] model for Cavco’s stock. I now estimate much stronger FY2025 revenue growth than previously at 12% given the company’s recent momentum and production ramp-up. Afterwards, due to industry outlook uncertainty, I have slightly lowered my expectations. I now estimate a FY2024-FY2034 CAGR of 6.0% with 2% perpetual growth afterwards.

With capacity utilization eventually ramping up, I again estimate Cavco’s EBIT margin to improve to a 13.1% level from a 9.9% achieved figure in FY2024. The company’s cash flow conversion should be good as the company should be able to maintain good manufacturing capacity without significant investments.

The estimates put Cavco’s fair value at $424.1, 11% below the stock price at the time of writing. As Cavco’s valuation has continued to get more expensive against homebuilder peers, the stock is getting too expensive. The DCF model now estimates moderate downside even in a scenario with great earnings growth.

The fair value estimate is up from $408.76 previously.

CAPM

The DCF model uses a cost of capital [WACC] of 10.14%, derived from a capital asset pricing model:

I still don’t expect Cavco to leverage debt. For cost of equity, I use the 10-year bond yield of 4.33% as the risk-free rate. The equity risk premium of 4.33% is Professor Aswath Damodaran’s most recent January estimates for the US. I have kept the beta estimate at 1.25. With a liquidity premium of 0.4%, the cost of equity and WACC both stand at 10.14%.

Takeaway

Cavco has performed incredibly well in recent quarters against a more challenging industry backdrop. The positioning in affordable housing seems to be aiding the company. Still, despite a great recent performance, the industry still has uncertainty in persisting high mortgage rates and the uncertain impact of tariffs. As Cavco’s stock has appreciated to quite an expensive level, I believe that the stock is becoming unattractive. Yet, as the estimated overvaluation is still moderate, I keep my rating on Cavco Industries at Hold for now.

data.title: AT&T: Don't Get FOMO At Peak Valuation
data.description:
AT&T (T) stock has given returns in the past few quarters which is higher than most of the bullish estimates. This year has been particularly good for the company as the wider S&P 500 went into a correction mode due to the tariff situation. However, this has also reduced the dividend attractiveness of AT&T, with the stock giving close to a 4% yield, which is lower than the 10-year treasury yield. The convergence of fiber and mobility service is a positive trend, but the fiber revenue is only $2 billion in 4Q24 compared with $16.6 billion in mobility service revenue. In the previous article, it was mentioned that it might be time to take some profits off the table.

Wall Street might become more positive towards AT&T stock compared with other companies if the tariff uncertainty increases. However, investors should not get FOMO for AT&T stock at the current valuation, which is close to the highest level in the recent past. AT&T is expected to deliver YoY revenue growth of only 1%-2% for the next few years. AT&T is expected to deliver EPS of $2.26 for the fiscal year ending Dec 2026, giving the stock a forward PE of 12.19. This might look modest, but we need to add the massive debt burden on the company to the calculation and find the multiple for the entire enterprise value. After the recent correction in Mag 7 stocks, companies like Meta (META) and Google (GOOG) (GOOGL) are also trading at forward P/E multiples in the teens, and they are still expected to deliver better growth in the longer term.

Prevent FOMO at peak valuation

AT&T has shown a massive bull run since hitting a bottom in mid-2023. Some of this bull run was helped by the overall bullish run in S&P 500 in 2024. The biggest divergence in returns of AT&T and S&P 500 started in 2025. This year has been particularly brutal for Mag 7 stocks. The recent tariff uncertainty has also hit many of them. However, this has changed the valuation dynamics between AT&T and some of the bigger tech stocks. AT&T’s valuation multiple after including the massive debt pile does not look very attractive when compared with some of the other tech stocks.

After the recent correction, the EV to EBIT multiple of Meta is 17.14, and for Alphabet it is 15.08. At the same time, AT&T’s EV to EBIT multiple is 14.10, which is modestly cheaper than the other two stocks. On the other hand, the YoY revenue growth of Meta and Alphabet is significantly higher. AT&T’s revenue growth is barely 1%, and in the next few years, AT&T is expected to deliver 1%-2% revenue growth.

After the recent stock run in AT&T, it is unclear if the bullish momentum can be maintained. Many investors are looking at AT&T as a safe haven option during tariff uncertainty. However, the dividend yield of 4.1% is less than the 10-year treasury yield. This further reduces the attraction of this stock. It should also be noted that AT&T stock did not perform particularly well during the Great Recession of 2008. AT&T stock fell from a peak of $31 in 2007 to a low of $17 by 2009. If we are going to see a massive economic slowdown or even a recession, AT&T might not be the best defense in this scenario.

In the above chart, we can see AT&T matched the S&P 500 decline during the Great Recession, while Walmart(WMT) was a better defensive stock.

Improving convergence and margins

AT&T has shown a strong convergence of its mobility and fiber business. This has allowed the company to increase the monetization of its current customer base. In the previous 4Q 2024 earnings, AT&T reported 17.8% YoY growth in the fiber business. This growth rate is only seen in the big tech companies. Many bullish analysts have pointed to this convergence as the key factor that can drive AT&T’s stock price.

However, it should be noted that the fiber business still forms a small fraction of the overall revenue base. In the previous quarter, AT&T delivered $16.6 billion in mobility service revenue, which had 3.3% YoY growth. On the other hand, the fiber revenue was $2 billion or 12% of the mobility service revenue base. It will take a long time for fiber to increase its revenue share and impact on the overall revenue growth.

Figure: Recent revenue and growth in mobility and fiber business. Source: Company Filings

Buybacks and a hold rating

AT&T’s management has already announced a buyback program of $10 billion in the previous quarter. It has been mentioned that the company has a capacity for a total of $20 billion buybacks between 2025-2027. This is equal to almost 10% of the market cap. We could see 3%-3.5% CAGR EPS growth from the buybacks alone. In addition, the company is giving a 4% dividend yield, which might increase in the near term. The steady decline in its long-term debt level is also a positive. However, investors should note that AT&T does not have many levers to pull in order to increase revenue growth. The consensus estimate for YoY revenue growth in the next three years is 1.25%, 1.20%, and 1.78% respectively.

Figure: Forward revenue growth estimate of AT&T. Source: Seeking Alpha

As mentioned above, the valuation multiple is not very attractive when compared with some of the bigger tech stocks. If an economic slowdown occurs in the next few quarters, AT&T could show a dip in revenue growth and margins. There have been several positive factors working in AT&T’s favor in the past few quarters, but they have been priced in by the recent bull run. This makes the stock a Hold at the current price.

Investor Takeaway

AT&T stock has shown close to 20% growth in YTD compared with a 10% dip in the S&P 500. However, this performance has made AT&T stock pricey compared with its historical average. AT&T is trading at a EV/EBIT ratio of 14.1 compared with Alphabet’s 15 and Meta’s 17.1. Both Alphabet and Meta have significantly higher revenue and EPS growth momentum than AT&T. The dividend yield of AT&T is also lower than the 10-year treasury, which further reduces the attraction of the stock.

The convergence of mobility and fiber has certainly helped AT&T in the last few quarters. It could continue to deliver better monetization of the current customer base. But the YoY revenue growth of AT&T is still expected to be a mere 1%-2% in the next few years. Investors should wait for a better entry point in AT&T as the stock seems to be close to its peak valuation.

data.title: Applied Materials Vs. ASML: The Ideal Pick During This Cyclical Downturn
data.description:
The base of this cyclical industry

As two of the most important semiconductor FAB equipment names, both Applied Materials (NASDAQ:AMAT) and ASML Holding (NASDAQ:ASML) have been hit hard by the cyclical downturn in Information Technology in general. Applied Materials has sold at a much cheaper valuation for the past few years. That doesn't necessarily make it a better buy off the bat; growth rates matter and the market will both pay up for growth and higher value-added products.

We can see that gross margin-wise, ASML does command a margin advantage in gross margins over Applied Materials. Again, this indicates a higher value on intellectual property and a wider moat for ASML.

On a 5-year basis, we also see much more total demand growth for ASML's products as evidenced by the higher growth rate in revenues as the two have seen a separation in trajectories around the 2023 mark. I guess the question to be asked is: ASML becoming the NVIDIA (NVDA), if you will, of the FAB equipment IT sub-sector with more replacement or substitute products available to compete with Applied Materials?

This article will do an in-depth analysis to figure out which one is a better buy at this moment in time based on value, balance sheet, and potential catalysts.

Year-to-date performance of both

Year to date, Applied Materials has seen a bigger sell-off than ASML, but both are performing poorly in line with the market in general.

Percent off high

Both are down -40+% from their all-time highs. Of the two, I've only covered AMAT previously, which is still positive on a total return basis:

In cyclical stocks, for which semiconductors are historically cyclical, buying such names when all the news is bad news is usually the best way to play them. This could be a great buy-the-dip moment for all semiconductor-related names in general, even if their forward numbers are hard to predict.

The differences in what these two equipment heavyweights do

Data from Seeking Alpha and Company Related Filings

Applied Materials ASML
Equipment is used for many steps of the chip-making process, including the transfer of patterns into device structures, transistor and interconnect fabrication, metrology, inspection and review, and packaging technologies for connecting finished IC dies. This is before and after lithography, manipulating wafer materials. Lithography and light-based patterning (DUV and EUV). These machines can produce much denser (3nm and denser) chips using lithography and have a near monopoly on this technology.
The company offers a wider variety of semiconductor fabricating equipment. The company produces mainly lithography machines.
Makes revenue from equipment and software sales Revenue from equipment, and software
$112 B market cap $257 B market cap

YOY revenue growth

4.34%

 YOY revenue growth 17.67%

Important commentary from ASML earnings

Firstly, ASML has the most recent earnings report and guidance, having reported on April 16th. They are and should be looked at as a leading indicator of how semiconductor demand and production look in general because you are not making the most advanced chips without their products.

Some earnings highlight from Seeking Alpha:

- The company reiterated its 2025 revenue guidance of €30 billion to €35 billion, with a gross margin expectation of 51%-53%. The full-year growth is expected to be supported by Logic and Memory demand, with AI-related applications being a leading growth driver.

- Management expressed confidence in 2026 being a growth year but acknowledged uncertainties due to macroeconomic conditions and tariff-related complexities.

- CEO Christophe Fouquet highlighted strong first-quarter financial results, with total net sales of €7.7 billion, aligned with guidance, and a gross margin of 54%, exceeding expectations. He emphasized the growing demand for AI-related semiconductor technologies as a key driver of growth for the company.

Even in the midst of tariffs and global trade turmoil, ASML was brave enough to reiterate earnings and have a positive outlook after a beat. If there is one product that the world seems to have an insatiable demand for at the moment, it's semiconductor FABs, chips, and GPUs.

Trade restrictions are the only thing limiting or capping sales at this time. It provides a lot of uncertainty, but the demand story remains and CAPEX spend predictions for the MAG 7 ex Microsoft remain very strong. They should be looked at as the end of the supply chain/ultimate customer for the products produced by these two FAB equipment names.

Weaker-than-expected booking guidance still weighed on the share price post-earnings. Much of this may have to do with general investment uncertainty rather than a true demand problem. The market needs clarity on tariffs and export regulations before better guidance can be had.

ASML 10-year growth lines

ASML still trades above its 10-year growth rate for normalized EPS, free cash flow, and revenue growth. The disparity is not dramatic, but it is there.

AMAT 10 year growth lines

Applied Materials now has the 10-year EPS growth line crossing above the price growth line, a positive value indicator. Price appreciation remains above other profitability metrics, but AMAT wins this comparison for being cheap on the chart.

ASML vs. AMAT expected growth rate and PEG ratio analysis

When looking at industries that are cyclical, higher expected growth rates are probably more important than absolute cheapness based on earnings multiples alone.

Here is the advice I've used regarding the PEG ratio from Peter Lynch:

In general, a p/e ratio that's half the growth rate is very positive, and one that's twice the growth rate is very negative. Lynch, Peter. One Up On Wall Street. 1989 (199)

What has the growth rate in earnings been in recent years? (My favorites are those in the 20 to 25% range. I'm wary of companies that seem to be growing faster than 25%. Those 50 percents usually are found in hot industries, and you know what that means.) Lynch, Peter. One Up On Wall Street. 1989 (229).

Thus, a PEG ratio of 1 would be when the P/E multiple and the expected growth rate are equivalent. The further below 1, the better the deal, meaning the growth rate is higher than the EPS multiple.

Right now, ASML is trading at under 25 X forward GAAP P/E and is expected to grow earnings by 34% this year and 16% next year, according to analyst estimates. The average growth rate for 2025 and 2026 would be right at 25%. Thus, paying nearly 25 X GAAP or non-GAAP for ASML, which has a more monopolistic product, is probably a better value at this moment than Applied Materials, which is seen as having more competition and only an 8.11% 2025 and 7.73% expected 2026 earnings growth rates.

In this case, the growth rate and reasonable multiple trump the absolute cheap multiple in Applied Materials combined with their low growth rate.

Edge to ASML.

Dividend yields

Both are cyclical and not of the aristocrat/king variety, nor should we expect them to be. They pay modest dividends with really good growth rates. Both have great balance sheets and these are currently very sustainable, with each paying out less than 30% of earnings. ASML has a higher growth rate, and AMAT has a slightly higher yield.

This one is a tie.

ASML balance sheet

  • Cash and short-term investments are 2.7 X greater than long-term debt, excellent.
  • Shares outstanding are down and to the right, excellent.
  • ASML only borrows (long-term debt) an amount equivalent to 43% of EBITDA, excellent.

AMAT balance sheet

  • Cash and short-term investments are 1.5 X greater than long-term debt, excellent.
  • Shares outstanding are down and to the right, excellent.
  • AMAT only borrows (long-term debt) an amount equivalent to 71% of EBITDA, excellent.

Conclusion

Both balance sheets are amazing and enviable. However, ASML has the better metrics, even though both are outstanding.

Edge goes to ASML

My final thoughts

ASML has the technology companies want now to print more and more, smaller and smaller transistors onto a chip. Applied Materials has a more diverse set of equipment offerings, including an EUV offering that could one day be an alternative. Currently, my understanding is that Centura Tetra (AMAT's EUV tech) is complementary to what ASML machines do, and not competitive, but having EUV tech in some capacity could mean AMAT makes a challenger machine to ASML in the future.

That challenge is not there yet, as evidenced by the growth rates in earnings and revenue, but it exists.

Tariffs could be a bigger headwind for ASML for US sales

Not clear is if tariffs will hamper the cost of machines and where they are made. ASML is a Netherlands-based company, and parts of its supply chain and components that go into its machines are sourced from places outside of the United States. From my understanding, more of the supply chain in regard to AMAT's equipment is US-based (they are a US-based company but have some operations abroad), and AMAT should be looked at as more tariff-proof if we're looking at US semi-FAB build-outs and who will be less affected or who may have to eat margin cost in order to make numbers work for their customer.

Taiwan tensions could be a risk factor

For both ASML and AMAT, the risk factor that Taiwan becomes a permanent Mainland China territory in the near future is a risk for both. If that were to occur, I imagine sanctions would not allow either to service existing machines or sell new ones into one of the biggest markets for their FAB equipment in the world.

Trump is not a fan of the Chips Act

On top of risks to international markets, Trump is no fan of the Chips Act and the stipulations that come along with the money being granted to applicants. Just how Trump intends to stimulate semiconductor production is still unclear. Perhaps he feels that many of these companies should just use their balance sheets and make all the investments themselves without assistance. I'm not confident that will happen (regarding FABs).

In the meantime, you have US-based semiconductor FAB builders like Intel (INTC) and Wolfspeed Inc. (WOLF) that are not in a great position to keep spending. It's going to have to be the foreign operators or semiconductor design companies that are flush with cash to make the investments. I would not count out Amazon (AMZN), Google (GOOGL), or Meta (META) getting involved on their own either. They have the cash and are way under-leveraged. Trump may be aware of just how much cash the Mag 7 are sitting on and wants to make them foot the bill.

Furthermore, while data center spending still seems on track, auto sales and auto tariffs could limit new vehicle production. This is also a chip-intensive market that could lead to less demand as well.

A US-China trade deal is also paramount

Finally, the US and China need to negotiate a trade deal that locks in certainty around what chip tariffs will be. This goes both ways, as China will not reduce tariffs on the US without the US first reducing tariffs on China. Chip companies that sell a lot of products to China, including AMAT and to a lesser extent ASML being that they have production outside the US, will also see less demand through these reciprocal tariffs. If Trump doesn't subsidize this industry somehow and limits the sale of their products in China, then revenues across the sector will see major pressure.

Who wins for value, balance sheet management, and catalyst factors?

ASML wins for better growth rates, margins, and balance sheets. AMAT is absolutely cheaper in a value stock sense, but the market is convinced that ASML has the more irreplaceable technology. In all honesty, both are looking like great buys as the IT sector bottoms out in the midst of trade war uncertainty. Both have the entire semiconductor FAB industry as their TAM and customer base; both also do different steps in the chip production process, so this is not a winner-take-all scenario.

However, with both down more than -40% from all-time highs, ASML is clearly the leader of the pack in the FAB equipment space at the moment and if I had to choose one, it would be the more monopolistic of the sub-sector as long as the multiples are not too outlandish, which they are not.

The US, along with TSMC, Intel, and possibly NVIDIA, either in joint projects with TSMC or on their own, will be incentivized to build out new and larger FABs to try to match what Taiwan is able to output. It's a tall task, but a lot of money will be spent on semiconductor FAB equipment in the process.

Many risks surround the semiconductor sector as a whole, but that's also a buying opportunity. No matter what happens in the world, I'm confident demand for these products will only increase over time.

data.title: VRIG: A Bit Disappointing (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Thesis

This year has proven to be a very tough one for many asset classes, including short-term bond funds. So far, we have the following tally in the markets:

The S&P 500 is down -10% this year, the Nasdaq is down -13%, while the equal weighted Invesco S&P 500 Equal Weight ETF (RSP) is down -7%. The aggregate bond market is slightly up, as illustrated by the iShares Core U.S. Aggregate Bond ETF (AGG).

What was peculiar about this year was the very violent sell-off in the past month driven by tariff announcements. The sell-off saw both equities and long bonds lose value, with a rare positive correlation.

Short-term bond funds were not spared, and in today's article we are going to re-visit the Invesco Variable Rate Investment Grade ETF (VRIG), a fund that we last covered two years ago.

Stressed markets drawdown

Let us have a look at how the name did in the recent market stress:

We are comparing VRIG with a cohort formed by the PIMCO Enhanced Short Maturity Active Fund (MINT), the T. Rowe Price Ultra Short-Term Bond ETF (TBUX), the iShares Short Duration Bond ETF (NEAR), the Janus Henderson AAA CLO ETF (JAAA) and the iShares Treasury Floating Rate Bond ETF (TFLO).

AAA CLOs had by far the largest drawdown, seeing figures at -1.6%. Surprisingly, however, VRIG had the second-largest drawdown, still being down -0.46% in the past month. As expected, pure treasuries were flat, while NEAR is a nice surprise, having a de-minimis drawdown. When compared to overweight bond funds, VRIG disappointed. We are going to have another look at the fund's current holdings and try to identify the factors behind this underperformance.

Current holdings

We are surprised because the fund has a portfolio duration of only 0.22 years, which should keep drawdowns very well contained even in the worst market scenarios.

Since duration is low, and cannot account for nearly a -0.5% drawdown, let us look at the fund components:

  • Corporate bonds: 42%
  • Securitized: 32%
  • Treasuries: 26%

Since corporate bonds have proven to be very liquid, even in this year's drawdown, we are of the opinion that there was slippage in the 'Securitized' bucket when it came down to prices. While the fund is not overweight CLOs in its 'Securitized' bucket, it does contain a large portion of ABS deals, which tend to be more illiquid during market sell-off events.

As a retail investor, it is quite impossible to assess the liquidity of each underlying holding in the fund, thus one has to rely on duration and ratings, but both those elements are looking good for the name:

  • AAA: 39%
  • AA: 15%
  • A: 33%
  • BBB: 11%
  • Other: residual

The fund is overweight in the top portion of the investment grade matrix, thus does not show any glaring issues. We are of the opinion that lower rated deals in the securitized category saw price slippage due to liquidity and wide bid/ask spreads.

Yield considerations

Investors buy short-dated bond funds because they provide for an enhanced yield versus simple treasury or T-Bill funds. VRIG falls in this category:

  • 30-day SEC yield: 4.94%
  • Portfolio yield to worst: 5.6%

The fund therefore offers a 60 bps additional yield when compared to pure T-bill funds. The recent drawdown in the name shows us that the yield enhancement is not for free, with the drawdown roughly equaling the incremental rate. In other words, if an investor panics and sells during a risk-off event, they give up all the additional yield for an entire year.

While the risk/reward ratio is fair, an investor needs to keep that in mind and understand that the additional 60 bps are not necessarily free and come with an increased drawdown risk.

Analytics

  • AUM: $1.2 billion.
  • Sharpe Ratio: 0.8 (3Y).
  • Std. Deviation: 1.2 (3Y).
  • Yield: 4.94%.
  • Premium/Discount to NAV: 0%.
  • Z-Stat: n/a.
  • Leverage Ratio: 0%.
  • Effective Duration: 0.22 years
  • Composition: Short-Term Corporate Bonds

Why the downgrade?

While in a range bound or risk-on market VRIG's drawdown profile is inconsequential, in volatile times it matters. As we have seen recently, the fund can see drawdowns and when compared to a cohort of peers, it has performed a bit subpar. While overall a -0.5% figure is very small, kindly keep in mind many investors bucket this fund as cash, thus might be surprised to see negative figures associated with it. The worst mistake to make is to sell the fund during a risk-off event, a decision which would erase all additional yield for an entire year.

Given the slim margin over T-bills, we no longer find VRIG to be a 'Buy', especially when considering a macro environment fraught with volatility. Until we get some clarity on tariffs and other points from President Trump's agenda, we are going to carefully analyze the risk and rewards associated with any name that engages in yield enhancement strategies.

Conclusion

VRIG is a fixed income exchange-traded fund. The name offers roughly 60 bps in yield over T-bills, and has performed admirably in calm markets. With the recent market sell-off, we saw the fund experience a -0.5% drawdown, much higher than its peers. While the fund duration is still very low at 0.22 years, the name is now running a bit of liquidity risk via its ABS sleeve, quite similar to what we saw with AAA CLOs. We are therefore downgrading the name to 'Hold' given the view that volatility will not go away this year and the fund will experience further drawdowns.

data.title: Warning: These REITs Are A Dumpster Fire In 2025
data.description:
I am very bullish on REITs (VNQ), and this is no secret to anyone who follows me here on Seeking Alpha. I write weekly bullish articles on various REITs, and this is where I am replying most of my capital at the moment.

However, the REIT sector is vast, and not all REITs are worth buying. Today, we will look at four categories of REITs that I am avoiding because they are either too risky or overpriced. Stick till the end as the one may surprise you.

Hotel REITs

The latest data shows that advanced flight bookings from Canadians coming to the US are down 70% year over year, and tourism from Europe is down 20-50% depending on the country.

Foreigners are showing less interest in coming to the US to spend their money there due to the perception that they are not being respected by the current administration.

On top of that, the threats of major tariffs, coupled with big government cuts, could push the economy into a recession.

This is very bad news for hotels, and it explains why most hotel REITs have been selling off lately.

It is the most cyclical sector of the commercial real estate market because it relies on discretionary spending, and there aren't any long-term leases to protect you.

Making matters worse, this is a sector that requires a lot of capex, and it is facing a growing threat from new technologies like Airbnb (ABNB).

This explains why, historically, the hotel REIT sector has been one of the least rewarding of the entire REIT market:

There will come a point when valuations are low enough to warrant investments into this sector, but we are not quite there yet.

If hotel REITs like Apple Hospitality (APLE) or Host Hospitality (HST) drop by another 10-20%, I will take a closer look.

Mortgage REITs

Mortgage REITs are very popular amongst individual investors because they always offer high dividend yields. To give you an example, AGNC (AGNC) is currently offering a 15% dividend yield!

But if it sounds too good to be true, it probably is, and this is the case here.

Mortgage REITs have performed very poorly over the long run. They would pay big dividends but suffer major capital losses at the same time, resulting in poor total returns:

The main reason for this is that they are commonly overleveraged and too heavily exposed to macro factors that are unpredictable and out of their control.

Small changes to interest rates or spreads can make or break their businesses, but it is near impossible to predict major turns in these factors.

Today, especially, the world we live in is very uncertain, and I wouldn't want the success of my investment to depend on what will happen to interest rates next.

In that sense, investing in mortgage REITs feels more like speculating than investing.

I would add that the surge in interest rates is now starting to put severe stress on even the best mortgage REITs. Even Arbor Realty (ABR) was recently forced to cut its dividend, and I suspect that it may need to cut it again in the near term. A lot of their loans are underwater, and they will likely face significant losses before they eventually recover.

I have no interest in this space.

Office REITs

Now, I am not in the camp of people who claim that offices are going obsolete.

I strongly believe that offices are here to stay, and I am saying this as someone who is running a small business entirely remotely.

I have seen and experienced both, so I know firsthand that there are pros and cons to each.

Offices will increase productivity and collaboration in most people, and it is especially important for a growing company that is training new people.

Therefore, offices are here to stay. There is no doubt about that as more and more big companies like Amazon (AMZN) are now bringing their employees back to the office.

But so why am I not buying office REITs?

They have simply recovered too quickly. As an example, SL Green (SLG) has nearly doubled investors' money over the past two and a half years as it has recovered.

While offices are here to stay, I still think that the sector will face significant pain for many years to come.

Most companies will keep an office but lease less space going forward.

This has put the office market into a severe state of oversupply, with the highest vacancy rates ever recovered at over 20%.

I expect a lot of the lower-quality buildings to change hands in the coming years as the current owners default on them, and as new owners come in with low bases, they will heavily reinvest in them to compete with the latest Class A properties.

Therefore, REITs like SL Green may have avoided most of the pain so far, but I still expect them to be impacted by the oversupply in the years ahead.

Valuations are just too high when taking this into account. I would much rather invest in other property sectors.

Data Center REITs

This one may surprise you.

It seems as if everyone wants to invest in data centers right now to benefit from the growing use of AI, but there's a problem with this.

Data center REITs are pricey.

Increasingly, many of these AI companies want to build their own data centers.

There is a significant risk of oversupply, especially following the latest news of new AI models requiring far less equipment.

Things are just changing too quickly, and I cannot assess whether today's data centers will still be needed to the same degree 20 years from now.

With so much money being pumped into the sector, valuations so high, and technologies evolving so quickly, I just feel that the risk-to-reward of REITs like Iron Mountain (IRM) isn't attractive.

Closing Note

The nice thing about investing in REITs today is that the entire sector is heavily discounted, and therefore, you don't need to invest in these risky sectors to find good value.

Even things like apartment REITs, industrial REITs, and healthcare REITs are heavily discounted.
